items.title: Coin-flip bias after 10 trials
items.scenario: You observe 7 heads in 10 independent flips. The Bernoulli likelihood is L(p) = p^7 (1−p)^3. Values of p near 0.7 score higher than p = 0.2 or p = 0.9.
items.explanation: Data are fixed (7 heads, 3 tails); you evaluate how well each candidate bias p explains that exact outcome.

items.title: Spam filter log-loss
items.scenario: A logistic model outputs p = 0.85 for a message labeled spam (y = 1). The Bernoulli log-likelihood contribution is log(0.85) ≈ −0.16; a wrong p = 0.10 would contribute log(0.10) ≈ −2.30.
items.explanation: Training minimizes the sum of negative log-likelihood terms—each term is log L(p) for one labeled example.

items.title: Gaussian Naive Bayes class score
items.scenario: For class “setosa,” feature petal length x = 1.4 is compared using the Normal PDF N(μ_setosa, σ²_setosa). That PDF value is one factor in the class-conditional likelihood for the flower.
items.explanation: GNB multiplies prior × product of per-feature Gaussian likelihoods; each PDF is f(x | μ, σ²) with x observed and parameters from training.

items.title: Poisson call-center counts
items.scenario: On four days you observe calls y = [3, 5, 4, 6]. With Poisson rate λ, L(λ) = ∏ e^{−λ} λ^{y_i} / y_i!. The MLE λ̂ is the sample mean 4.5.
items.explanation: The Poisson PMF is evaluated at each observed count; the product over days is the joint likelihood for IID Poisson data.

items.title: Softmax digit classifier
items.scenario: MNIST label is digit 3. Model outputs π = [0.05, 0.02, 0.01, 0.88, …]. The Categorical likelihood for the true class is L = π_3 = 0.88; loss is −log(0.88).
items.explanation: Softmax probabilities parameterize the Categorical likelihood; cross-entropy is negative log-likelihood.
